symptom: Retention times shifted earlier than expected
likely_causes: column degradation
mobile phase composition change
temperature fluctuation
diagnostics: check column age and void volume
verify mobile phase batch
check column oven temperature log
corrective_actions: replace column
prepare fresh mobile phase
recalibrate column oven
escalation_conditions: shift > 10% of expected RT
multiple analytes affected simultaneously

symptom: Gradual loss of sensitivity over multiple injections
likely_causes: matrix buildup on source
dirty spray shield
blocked capillary
diagnostics: inspect spray shield visually
run solvent blank and compare background
check capillary condition
corrective_actions: clean source components
replace spray shield
flush capillary with strong solvent
escalation_conditions: sensitivity loss > 50%
source cleaning does not restore signal

symptom: Unexpected peaks appearing in blank injections
likely_causes: contaminated inlet liner
septum bleed
column bleed
solvent impurity
diagnostics: run solvent blank
bake out column at high temperature
replace inlet liner and septum
corrective_actions: replace liner and septum
bake out column
use higher purity solvent
escalation_conditions: ghost peaks co-elute with target analytes
persist after liner replacement

symptom: Noisy or drifting mass signal during TGA measurement
likely_causes: external vibration from HVAC, foot traffic, or nearby equipment
air drafts reaching the balance chamber
static electricity on sample or ceramic crucible
contaminated or mechanically damaged hang-down wire
sample mass exceeding balance capacity (>1 g)
diagnostics: run an empty-crucible baseline scan and quantify baseline noise level
verify purge gas flow rate (typically 40–60 mL/min N2 balance + 60 mL/min furnace)
inspect hang-down wire under magnification for contamination, kinks, or corrosion
temporarily close off lab HVAC and check if noise improves
verify instrument is on a vibration-isolated bench or anti-vibration pad
corrective_actions: relocate instrument away from vibration sources; use vibration-isolation pad
shield instrument from air drafts; maintain stable purge gas flow rate
clean hang-down wire with isopropanol-wetted lint-free swab; allow to dry fully
reduce sample mass to 5–20 mg for optimal signal-to-noise
replace hang-down wire if corrosion or mechanical damage is confirmed
escalation_conditions: noise exceeds 0.01 mg RMS after all corrective actions
hang-down wire visibly broken, fused, or severely corroded
balance drift > 0.5 mg/min on empty crucible baseline

symptom: Onset or decomposition temperature does not match expected literature or previous results
likely_causes: temperature sensor drift requiring recalibration
heating rate too fast causing thermal lag between furnace and sample
temperature calibration not performed with certified Curie-point standards
sample mass too large (> 20 mg) introducing internal thermal gradients
furnace atmosphere (oxidising vs inert) not matching literature conditions
diagnostics: run Curie-point temperature calibration using certified standards (e.g., Alumel ~154°C, Nickel ~358°C, Iron ~780°C)
compare onset temperature at multiple heating rates (5, 10, 20 °C/min) to extrapolate to zero-rate
verify purge gas identity and flow rate match conditions of reference measurement
repeat measurement with 5 mg sample and compare onset with previous 20 mg run
review temperature calibration date in TA Instruments TRIOS software
corrective_actions: perform full temperature calibration using TA Instruments Curie-point standards kit
use slower heating rate (5–10 °C/min) for precise onset temperature determination
reduce sample mass to ≤ 10 mg to minimise thermal gradients
match purge gas and flow rate exactly to the published reference conditions
contact TA Instruments service if calibration does not hold between consecutive runs
escalation_conditions: temperature offset > 5°C after recalibration
Curie-point transitions no longer sharp (broad over > 10°C range)
furnace thermocouple visibly damaged or oxidised

symptom: Apparent mass gain at the beginning of heating or a systematic upward drift in mass with increasing temperature on an empty crucible
likely_causes: buoyancy effect: gas density decreases with rising temperature, reducing buoyant force on crucible
no baseline subtraction applied to sample run
using high-density inert gas (Argon) without buoyancy correction
gas flow rate changed between baseline and sample run
diagnostics: run an empty crucible baseline under identical conditions (same gas, flow, temperature programme)
check if apparent mass gain magnitude scales with gas density (Ar > N2 > He)
overlay sample curve with empty-crucible baseline to isolate true mass loss
verify gas flow rate is identical between baseline and sample measurement
corrective_actions: subtract empty-crucible baseline from sample data in TRIOS software
enable automatic buoyancy correction in TRIOS method settings
use helium purge if lowest buoyancy artifact is required
run baseline and sample at the same gas, flow rate, and temperature programme without exception
escalation_conditions: apparent mass gain > 0.5 mg on empty crucible after gas stabilisation
artifact prevents accurate mass loss determination for small events (< 1% mass loss)

symptom: Sample shows exothermic mass gain or oxidative decomposition under nominally inert (N2 or Ar) purge
likely_causes: insufficient purge time before starting ramp (residual O2 in furnace)
O2 leak through gas line fittings, tubing cracks, or loose connections
gas purity insufficient (< 99.999% N2 or Ar)
ambient air back-diffusion through furnace exhaust port
wrong gas cylinder connected to balance or furnace purge line
diagnostics: increase pre-run purge time to ≥ 15 minutes at high flow (100 mL/min) before starting
verify gas purity certificate; use only 99.999% (5.0 grade) N2 or Ar
inspect all gas line fittings with soapy water or leak detector spray for bubbles
run a blank with easily oxidised reference material (e.g., pure carbon black) to confirm inertness
check outlet of furnace for back-flow by holding a tissue near exhaust
corrective_actions: extend pre-run purge time to ≥ 15 minutes; use an oxygen scavenger on the gas line
replace gas tubing and tighten all Swagelok fittings; leak-test under pressure
switch to certified 99.999% inert gas cylinder from reputable supplier
install an O2 trap/purifier inline between the cylinder and instrument
add a positive pressure relief port to prevent air back-diffusion at exhaust
escalation_conditions: O2 contamination confirmed but source cannot be identified after full line inspection
furnace ceramic or thermocouple shows signs of oxidative damage
sample safety hazard due to unexpected combustion

symptom: Mass loss percentage varies significantly between replicate runs of the same material
likely_causes: sample inhomogeneity (particle size, mixing)
variable sample packing density in crucible
moisture or solvent uptake between sample preparation and loading
platinum or alumina crucible contamination from previous run
inconsistent sample mass between replicates
diagnostics: compare mass loss curves normalised to sample mass — are onset temperatures consistent?
check if variance correlates with sample particle size or preparation method
measure sample immediately after weighing to minimise moisture uptake
inspect crucibles for residue; run empty crucible through full temperature programme
record exact sample mass for each replicate and compare normalised curves
corrective_actions: grind and sieve sample to a uniform particle size (< 100 µm) before analysis
use consistent tamping/packing pressure when loading crucible
store prepared samples in a desiccator and analyse within 30 minutes of preparation
anneal platinum crucibles at 900°C for 30 min after each use to burn off residues
target the same sample mass (± 0.5 mg) for all replicates using a 5-place balance
escalation_conditions: RSD of mass loss % exceeds 5% across ≥ 5 replicates after addressing all corrective actions
onset temperature varies by > 10°C between replicates of the same sample

symptom: High-frequency noise or erratic fluctuations on DSC heat-flow baseline
likely_causes: electrical interference from power supply or nearby equipment
vibration transmitted to cell sensors
purge gas flow too high causing turbulence inside cell
loose or corroded electrical connections at cell
condensation inside DSC cell (sub-ambient measurements)
diagnostics: run an empty-cell baseline at the same conditions and quantify baseline noise (µW RMS)
compare noise at different scan rates (1, 5, 10, 20 °C/min) — thermal noise scales with rate
temporarily unplug nearby instruments and check if noise improves
inspect all cable connections from cell to electronic module
verify purge gas (N2 or He) flow rate is 50 mL/min or as specified in method
corrective_actions: connect instrument to a dedicated power circuit with line conditioner
check and firmly seat all cell electrical connections; clean with contact spray if corroded
set purge gas flow to 50 mL/min N2 or 25 mL/min He; avoid turbulent flow
for sub-ambient work: dry cell with dry N2 purge overnight; use dry gas generator
use lower heating rate to reduce inherent noise floor
escalation_conditions: baseline noise > 10 µW RMS at 10 °C/min after all corrective actions
cell sensor reads implausible temperature (> 10°C from setpoint at equilibrium)
visible moisture or contamination inside the DSC cell

symptom: Glass transition temperature (Tg) is lower or higher than expected or not reproducible between runs
likely_causes: different heating rate from literature reference (Tg shifts ~3–5°C per decade of heating rate)
thermal history not erased: residual stress or crystallinity from processing
residual moisture plasticising the polymer (lowers Tg)
temperature calibration offset (instrument Tg ≠ true Tg)
sample thickness too great causing thermal lag
diagnostics: perform heat-cool-heat cycle: first heat erases history, Tg from second heat is intrinsic
check and match heating rate exactly to the reference publication
dry sample in oven at T_onset - 30°C for 2h and re-measure immediately
verify temperature calibration using indium (156.6°C onset) and cyclohexane (-87°C) or other certified standard
reduce sample mass to ≤ 5 mg to minimise thermal lag
corrective_actions: always report Tg from second heating scan after controlled cooling at 10 °C/min
dry hygroscopic samples at 60–80°C in vacuum for ≥ 2h before DSC
recalibrate temperature axis using indium; use MDSC for weak Tg signals
standardise heating rate across all runs and always quote rate when reporting Tg
crimp hermetic pans to prevent moisture re-absorption between preparation and measurement
escalation_conditions: Tg variation > 5°C between replicates using the same thermal history protocol
indium calibration onset temperature deviates > 1°C from 156.6°C

symptom: Melting or crystallisation peak is unusually broad, split, or poorly resolved from baseline
likely_causes: poor thermal contact between sample and pan bottom (uneven sample distribution)
heating rate too fast for sample thermal mass
sample particle size too large causing internal temperature gradients
hermetic pan lid not crimped flat, creating air gap
sample contains a wide distribution of crystal sizes or polymorphs
diagnostics: press sample flat to ensure intimate pan contact; use spatula or press tool before crimping
repeat measurement at 2 and 5 °C/min and compare peak width — broad peaks narrow at slow rate
check lid crimp: a properly crimped pan has no gap visible around the edge
compare peak shape with known reference material (e.g., indium) to rule out instrument artefact
cross-section the sample pan after measurement and inspect sample distribution
corrective_actions: press powdered sample flat in pan with a micro-spatula before crimping lid
reduce heating rate to 2–5 °C/min for materials with high thermal mass (e.g., metals, ceramics)
reduce sample mass to 2–5 mg and increase sample surface-to-mass ratio
use TA Instruments hermetic pan crimping tool; inspect seal under microscope
for polymorphic samples: apply a standard annealing pre-treatment and document
escalation_conditions: peak width at half-height is > 5× broader than indium reference under same conditions
split peak cannot be narrowed by reducing sample mass or heating rate (suggests true polymorphism)

symptom: Calculated enthalpy (ΔH) values are inconsistent between replicates or deviate significantly from literature
likely_causes: enthalpy calibration not performed or performed with incorrect indium mass
pan mass mismatch between sample and reference pans (> 0.5 mg difference)
incorrect baseline integration limits applied in TRIOS software
sample reacts with pan material (e.g., corrosive samples in aluminium pans)
incomplete transition: sample not fully melted or crystallised within scan range
diagnostics: run indium calibration standard (> 3 mg, known literature ΔHf = 28.57 J/g) and check recovery
weigh sample and reference pans on a 5-place balance; pair pans within ± 0.3 mg
review integration baseline selection in TRIOS — use sigmoid baseline for overlapping transitions
check if sample reacts with pan (discolouration, pitting, or gas evolution) after run
extend temperature range to ensure complete transition before integration
corrective_actions: perform indium enthalpy calibration with fresh certified standard (minimum 3 mg accurately weighed)
use weight-matched sample and reference pans; pre-weigh and sort pans in batches
apply consistent baseline type (linear or sigmoid) across all replicates; document in SOP
switch to gold, platinum, or stainless-steel pans for reactive or corrosive samples
widen scan range by ±20°C beyond expected transition to capture full peak area
escalation_conditions: indium ΔH recovery < 95% or > 105% of 28.57 J/g after recalibration
enthalpy RSD > 5% across ≥ 5 replicates of same material and preparation

symptom: DSC baseline is curved, non-linear, or returns to a different level after a transition
likely_causes: scan rate too high for instrument thermal equilibration
asymmetric loading: sample and reference pans have different heat capacity or contact quality
cell contamination from previous run
pan contact problem — pan not sitting flat on sensor
heat capacity of sample changes significantly over the scanned range (common in polymers)
diagnostics: run empty-cell baseline and empty matched-pan baseline; overlay with sample run
check if curvature direction reverses when empty pans are swapped between sample and reference positions
inspect DSC cell for residue; clean with dry cotton swab if contaminated
reduce scan rate to 10 °C/min and re-run; excessive curvature at high rates suggests thermal asymmetry
in TRIOS, subtract the matched empty-pan run from the sample run as instrument baseline correction
corrective_actions: limit scan rate to ≤ 20 °C/min for standard measurements; use 10 °C/min for best baseline
use weight-matched pans in both sample and reference positions
clean DSC cell sensors with dry, lint-free cotton swab; use isopropanol only for persistent residue
ensure pans sit flat with no rocking — press gently with flat tool after placement
apply matched empty-pan baseline subtraction in TRIOS data analysis for precision work
escalation_conditions: baseline step after empty-pan subtraction > 1 mW at any scan rate ≤ 10 °C/min
residue in cell does not respond to routine cleaning (requires field service)

symptom: System pressure exceeds alarm limit or rises significantly during a run
likely_causes: column end-frit or pre-column filter blocked by precipitated protein or particulates
air bubble trapped inside column or in-line filter
column bed collapsed or heavily fouled
system tubing partially blocked (narrow-bore tubing after sample pump)
flow rate set above column manufacturer maximum or above pump specification
diagnostics: measure pressure drop across each component individually by bypassing column, inline filter, and UV cell
check if pressure drops when flow rate is halved (pressure should drop proportionally if column is clear)
run 1 CV of CIP buffer (e.g., 0.5 M NaOH for HIC/IEX, 6 M guanidine HCl for affinity) at 50% flow
inspect inline A1/B1 inlet filters for discolouration or blockage
review UNICORN pressure log to identify when pressure started rising (gradual vs. sudden)
corrective_actions: replace or clean inline 0.2 µm filter upstream of column
perform column CIP according to resin manufacturer protocol (Cytiva column handbook)
back-flush column at low flow rate (0.5 mL/min) to dislodge frit blockage if protocol allows
reduce flow rate to ≤ 75% of column maximum; increase run time to compensate
replace column if CIP does not restore pressure to < 80% of alarm limit
escalation_conditions: pressure alarm triggers during equilibration with clean buffer (indicates permanent column damage)
system pressure does not drop when column is bypassed (system-level blockage requires service)
back-pressure exceeds pump maximum safe operating pressure

symptom: Target protein peaks poorly resolved or co-eluting with contaminants; broad asymmetric peaks
likely_causes: column void volume or cracked bed from overpressure or freeze-thaw damage
sample overloading: protein mass exceeds dynamic binding capacity
gradient slope too steep — insufficient time for selective elution
incorrect buffer pH or ionic strength for the selected resin
sample not fully soluble or aggregated prior to injection
diagnostics: inject a small volume of acetone or blue dextran and observe peak symmetry (As should be 0.8–1.2)
reduce sample load by 50% and compare resolution
run a scouting gradient (shallow 20–100% B over 30 CV) to identify optimal elution window
verify buffer pH with calibrated electrode; adjust ionic strength to match resin conditions
centrifuge sample at 10,000 × g for 5 min; filter through 0.2 µm before injection
corrective_actions: if peak asymmetry > 1.5: re-pack column or replace resin; do not operate with void volume
reduce sample load to ≤ 30% of measured dynamic binding capacity
flatten gradient: extend elution phase to ≥ 20 CV for improved resolution
re-prepare buffers at correct pH and conductivity as per resin datasheet
pre-filter and clarify sample; add 1–5% glycerol to stabilise aggregation-prone proteins
escalation_conditions: peak asymmetry (As) > 2.0 after sample reduction and gradient optimisation (column bed damage)
resolution does not improve after all buffer and loading optimisations

symptom: Erratic UV signal spikes, flow instability, or pressure drops suggesting air in the flow path
likely_causes: buffers not properly degassed (dissolved gases come out of solution under low pressure)
inlet filter (A1 or B1) partially blocked drawing air
buffer bottle emptied during run — pump draws air
system not fully primed after maintenance or tubing change
back-pressure regulator (BPR) set too low, allowing dissolved gas to de-gas in UV cell
diagnostics: visually inspect tubing for visible air pockets; check UV signal for sharp spikes
in UNICORN, run System > Prime All to fill all lines
check buffer volumes — ensure all inlets are fully submerged (not drawing air)
increase back-pressure regulator setting to 0.3–0.5 MPa to keep gases in solution
run a pump wash cycle and inspect flow from outlet for bubbles
corrective_actions: degas all buffers by vacuum filtration (0.2 µm) or sonication for ≥ 15 min before use
prime all pump channels in UNICORN (System > System wash / Prime) before starting run
increase back-pressure regulator to 0.3–0.5 MPa for aqueous buffers
replace A1/B1 inlet filters if flow rate is noticeably reduced
ensure buffer bottle levels are monitored; add level sensor alarm in UNICORN method
escalation_conditions: air bubble reaches column: pressure drops suddenly, re-equilibration required before proceeding
UV baseline does not stabilise after full prime and degassing — possible UV cell contamination

symptom: UV detector baseline (280 nm or 260 nm) is noisy, drifting, or shows erratic spikes during blank run
likely_causes: air bubble passing through UV flow cell
UV lamp nearing end of life (intensity too low)
UV flow cell contaminated with protein residue or lipids
UV flow cell path length incorrect for expected protein concentration
buffer components absorbing at monitored wavelength (e.g., imidazole absorbs at 280 nm)
diagnostics: run a buffer blank through the UV cell and record baseline noise (should be < 2 mAU at 280 nm)
in UNICORN, check UV lamp intensity value — below 200 (instrument-dependent) indicates lamp replacement needed
check if noise is reproducible and constant (contamination) vs. random spikes (air bubbles)
review buffer composition: imidazole > 50 mM absorbs at 280 nm; switch to 254 nm or 300 nm reference
inspect flow cell windows visually through the UV port if accessible
corrective_actions: eliminate air bubbles: increase back-pressure, degas buffers, prime lines (see FPLC air bubble protocol)
clean UV flow cell: inject 5 CV of 0.1 M NaOH, then 5 CV water, then equilibration buffer
replace UV lamp if intensity below manufacturer threshold (Cytiva service part)
switch monitoring wavelength: use 214 nm for all proteins; 254 nm when imidazole is present
use 10 mM instead of ≥ 50 mM imidazole in binding buffer where chromatography conditions allow
escalation_conditions: UV lamp replacement does not restore intensity — detector unit requires service
flow cell cleaning does not restore baseline — cell scratched or etched, requires replacement

symptom: Conductivity trace during gradient does not match expected profile; elution positions shift between runs
likely_causes: pump A or pump B flow accuracy has drifted — requires recalibration
air bubble in gradient mixer causing flow rate fluctuation
buffer composition error: incorrect salt concentration or wrong buffer prepared
gradient mixer volume not accounted for in method delay volume settings
valve actuation delay causing slow switchover between buffer A and B
diagnostics: run a step gradient (0%, 50%, 100% B) at low flow and compare conductivity to theoretically expected values
perform pump flow rate verification in UNICORN (System > Pump calibration test)
independently measure buffer conductivity with a benchtop conductivity meter and compare to ÄKTA reading
check UNICORN method for correct delay volume (gradient mixer volume + tubing volume) compensation
run gradient with fresh, freshly prepared buffers of verified composition
corrective_actions: recalibrate pumps A and B using UNICORN pump calibration wizard
prime gradient mixer thoroughly: run 5 CV 100% B, then 5 CV 100% A before starting gradient method
prepare fresh buffers with verified conductivity; confirm salt concentrations gravimetrically or by conductivity meter
update method delay volume in UNICORN to match actual system void volume (measure with acetone tracer)
check mixer seal integrity; replace mixer if step gradient still deviates > 2% from expected
escalation_conditions: conductivity deviation > 5% from expected at verified buffer concentrations after pump recalibration
pump calibration test fails repeatedly — mechanical pump wear, requires service engineer

symptom: Incomplete separation of full-length oligonucleotide from n-1 failure sequences or other impurities by IP-RP or IEX chromatography
likely_causes: gradient slope too steep for resolving single-nucleotide length differences
column temperature too low: elevated temperature improves selectivity for oligonucleotides
sample load too high — overloads ion-pair reversed-phase column
incorrect ion-pairing agent concentration (TEA, TEAA) or ACN gradient range
column not fully equilibrated with ion-pair buffer before injection
diagnostics: run a shallow scouting gradient (5–40% ACN over 60 min) at 50°C to locate the optimal elution window
compare resolution with and without ion-pairing agent (100 mM TEAA pH 7.0 is standard for IP-RP)
reduce sample load by 5-fold and compare peak widths — overloading confirms if peak broadening is load-dependent
verify column temperature is set to 50–60°C (elevated temperature is critical for oligo selectivity)
run blank gradient and check for ghost peaks from previous run (carryover of longer oligos)
corrective_actions: use shallower ACN gradient: 5–25% ACN over 40–60 CV with 100 mM TEAA pH 7.0 as ion-pair buffer
set column temperature to 50–60°C using ÄKTA column oven or external heated column compartment
reduce sample concentration and injection volume to ≤ 10% of column binding capacity
equilibrate column with ≥ 10 CV of 5% ACN / 100 mM TEAA before injection
for IEX mode: use NaCl or NaClO4 gradient on anion-exchange resin (e.g., Source 15Q) at pH 12
escalation_conditions: n and n-1 peaks cannot be resolved even at shallow gradient and reduced load (column selectivity too low for oligo length)
column pressure rises during ACN gradient (ACN incompatible with column — verify resin compatibility)

symptom: Low crude purity, deletion sequences detected by HPLC-MS, truncated peptide products
likely_causes: Insufficient coupling reagent equivalents (HATU/HBTU/DIC) for sterically hindered residues
Coupling time too short for difficult sequences (beta-sheet prone, hydrophobic stretches)
Resin overloading — substitution too high for efficient solvation
Aggregation of growing peptide chain blocking amino acid access
Degraded or improperly stored coupling reagents (HATU hygroscopic)
diagnostics: Perform Kaiser (ninhydrin) or chloranil test for free amines after each coupling
Run analytical HPLC-MS of resin cleavage aliquot to identify deletion positions
Check coupling reagent freshness and storage conditions (HATU: dry, below -20 C)
Measure resin substitution (Fmoc loading) by UV quantitation at 301 nm after Fmoc removal
corrective_actions: Double-couple difficult residues (Ile, Val, Thr, Aib) with fresh activator
Increase coupling time to 60-120 min for beta-sheet prone sequences
Use HATU/HOAt or PyBOP/HOBt with DIPEA in DMF — avoid HBTU for low-loading resins
Add pseudoproline (Psi Pro) dipeptide building blocks at Ser/Thr positions to disrupt aggregation
Reduce resin substitution: target 0.2-0.4 mmol/g for long or difficult sequences
Implement microwave-assisted coupling (50-75 C) to improve kinetics
escalation_conditions: Deletion sequences >20% by HPLC even after double coupling — consider redesigning sequence
Coupling efficiency <90% on multiple consecutive residues (systematic issue)

symptom: Resin bead clumping, incomplete swelling, poor coupling efficiency mid-sequence, gel-like resin
likely_causes: Beta-sheet aggregation from hydrophobic stretches (Leu, Ile, Val, Phe clusters)
Inappropriate solvent — DMF only without co-solvent to disrupt H-bonding
High resin substitution causing steric crowding on beads
Polar sequence segments forming intra-chain hydrogen bonds
diagnostics: Visually inspect resin: clumped or gel-like beads indicate aggregation
Try resin in DCM or NMP — improved swelling indicates DMF aggregation
Perform Fmoc loading quantitation mid-synthesis to detect coupling drop-off
Check solubility of peptide in synthesis solvent by cleaving a test aliquot
corrective_actions: Switch to NMP or DMF/DCM (1:1) with 10-30% DMSO to disrupt beta-sheet structure
Add chaotropic additives: 0.1 M LiCl or 0.3 M LiClO4 in DMF
Use backbone amide protection (Dmb on Gly/Ala) to disrupt H-bonding
Incorporate a beta-breaker residue (e.g. Aib) in the middle of hydrophobic runs
Reduce synthesis temperature and extend solvation times before coupling
escalation_conditions: Resin beads irreversibly fused — restart synthesis with lower substitution resin
No improvement with solvent change — consider Fmoc strategy on PEG-based resin (ChemMatrix)

symptom: Retained Fmoc detected by UV, low coupling on next residue, double-Fmoc peak in HPLC
likely_causes: Piperidine concentration too low or contact time insufficient
Hindered Fmoc group after Aib, N-methyl amino acids, or Hmb backbone protection
Aggregated chain making Fmoc inaccessible to piperidine
Piperidine solution degraded or containing dibenzofulvene build-up
diagnostics: Monitor UV absorbance at 301 nm during deprotection: incomplete if signal persists
Run double deprotection and compare UV response on second vs. first treatment
Kaiser test after deprotection — positive (blue) confirms free amine
Inspect piperidine solution for dibenzofulvene precipitate (turbidity)
corrective_actions: Use 20% piperidine in DMF for 2 x 5 min (standard); extend to 2 x 10 min for hindered residues
For N-Me amino acids: use 20% piperidine + 0.1 M HOBt in DMF to suppress diketopiperazine
Replace piperidine solution — dibenzofulvene accumulation inhibits deprotection
Use 5% DBU + 5% piperidine in DMF for very hindered Fmoc groups
For aggregated sequences: pre-swell resin in NMP with DMSO before deprotection
escalation_conditions: Incomplete deprotection after 3 extended treatments — likely Hmb or backbone protected residue
Systematic failure at same position in repeated synthesis attempts

symptom: Low cleavage yield, incomplete side-chain deprotection, tert-butyl or Pbf adducts in MS, peptide stuck on resin
likely_causes: TFA cleavage time too short for bulky protecting groups (Pbf on Arg requires over 2 h)
Insufficient scavenger cocktail — reattachment of carbocations to Trp or Cys
High loading or aggregated peptide with restricted TFA access
Low water or TIS content in cleavage cocktail preventing full deprotection
diagnostics: Analyse crude peptide by HPLC-MS: look for +56 Da (tBu adduct) or +252 Da (Pbf adduct) peaks
Re-treat resin with fresh TFA cocktail if first cleavage shows incomplete deprotection
Check cleavage cocktail composition against recommended protocols for side-chains present
Weigh resin before and after cleavage to assess remaining peptide load
corrective_actions: Extend TFA cleavage to 3-4 h for Arg-rich peptides (Pbf removal is rate-limiting)
Use Reagent K (TFA/water/thioanisole/EDT/phenol 82.5:5:5:2.5:5) for Cys/Met peptides
For simple sequences: TFA/TIS/H2O (95:2.5:2.5) for 2 h is adequate
Add 2-5% thioanisole or EDT to scavenge t-Bu carbocations and protect Trp
Filter resin and re-cleave residual peptide with fresh TFA cocktail for 30 min
escalation_conditions: Adducts still present after 4 h cleavage with excess scavengers — consider backbone protection
Peptide precipitates in diethyl ether — possible incomplete deprotection, re-dissolve in AcOH/water

symptom: Epimeric peptide impurities, doublet peaks in chiral HPLC, same mass as target with different retention time in MS
likely_causes: OBt ester activation of C-terminal amino acid loading — especially Cys, His, Thr
Excess base (DIPEA) and long activation time before coupling — oxazolone formation
Use of HBTU/HATU at elevated temperature without coupling time control
His coupling without Boc or Trt protection on imidazole side chain
diagnostics: Run chiral HPLC (Chirobiotic T or CHIRALPAK column) to detect D-enantiomers
HPLC-MS: same mass, earlier-eluting mirror-image peak on C18 reversed-phase
Check coupling protocol — HBTU/DIPEA >2 eq at RT for >2 min is racemization-prone for Cys
Verify His building block: use Fmoc-His(Trt)-OH or Fmoc-His(Boc)-OH
corrective_actions: Use HATU/HOAt instead of HBTU/HOBt — reduced racemization due to active ester stability
Minimise pre-activation time: add amino acid and activator to resin simultaneously
For Cys: use Fmoc-Cys(Trt)-OH and couple with PyBOP/HOBt/DIPEA at RT
For His: switch to Fmoc-His(Trt)-OH or add collidine as non-nucleophilic base
Reduce DIPEA to 2 equivalents and avoid excess base during activation
escalation_conditions: Racemisation >5% on multiple residues — reconsider activation strategy for entire sequence
Chiral HPLC required for final purity release if epimerisation confirmed

symptom: Missed amino acid delivery, clogged lines, incorrect volume dispensed, reagent not transferred to vessel
likely_causes: Clogged frit or delivery line from precipitated amino acid or activator
Gas bubble in syringe pump or delivery line interrupting flow
Incorrect volume calibration — syringe pump drift over time
Amino acid solution crystallised in lines due to temperature drop
Empty or low-volume amino acid vessel not detected by sensor
diagnostics: Run a priming cycle and visually check all delivery lines for flow
Weigh reaction vessels before and after delivery to confirm dispensed volume
Inspect frits and transfer lines for crystalline deposits or blockages
Check instrument log/event file for delivery alarms or flow errors
Perform calibration run with solvent only to verify syringe pump accuracy
corrective_actions: Back-flush clogged delivery lines with DMF or DMSO and sonicate if accessible
Prime all lines before synthesis to purge air bubbles
Recalibrate syringe pump volumes using gravimetric check
Increase amino acid solution temperature or switch to higher-solubility solvent (DMF/NMP)
Replace blocked frits per CSBio/CEM maintenance schedule
escalation_conditions: Repeated delivery failures on same channel — hardware fault, contact CSBio/CEM service
Syringe pump not responding to calibration — replace syringe or pump mechanism

symptom: GC oven not cooling, oven temperature remains high after program ends, cooling is slower than expected
likely_causes: oven cooling fan failure or fan speed reduction due to motor degradation
oven vent flap or cooling duct blocked by lint, debris, or mechanical obstruction
ambient laboratory temperature too high — insufficient temperature differential for fan-assisted cooling
electronic temperature controller fault: PID parameters drifted, controller board failure
auxiliary cryogenic cooling system failure: LN2 or CO2 solenoid valve not opening
diagnostics: listen for cooling fan operation immediately after oven cycle ends — no fan noise indicates fan failure
inspect rear oven vent outlet for blockage; check for detectable airflow with hand near exhaust
compare actual cool-down time against instrument specification (typical: 300°C to 50°C in 10–15 min)
check instrument diagnostic menu or front-panel display for oven temperature sensor error codes
verify ambient laboratory temperature is ≤ 30°C — high ambient significantly slows cooling
for cryogenic cooling: check LN2 or CO2 supply pressure and listen for solenoid valve actuation
corrective_actions: replace oven cooling fan if no airflow detected at exhaust vent; consult Agilent/Shimadzu/Thermo Fisher service manual for part number
clear oven vent blockage: remove back panel and clean vent duct with compressed air or soft brush
improve lab ventilation or reduce ambient temperature to below 30°C; relocate instrument away from heat sources
run instrument self-diagnostics from front panel or data system (Chemstation, OpenLAB, LabSolutions) to retrieve controller error codes
call service engineer if temperature controller error persists after power cycle — may require PID recalibration or board replacement
for LN2/CO2 cooling: verify solenoid valve opens during cool-down cycle; replace valve if stuck closed
escalation_conditions: oven temperature does not decrease below 100°C within 30 min of program end — severe cooling failure
instrument reports oven temperature sensor fault or hardware error on front panel display
oven fan replacement does not restore normal cooling — internal duct or controller fault requires service engineer

symptom: GC oven temperature overshooting setpoint, temperature oscillation, or failure to hold isothermal temperature accurately
likely_causes: PID controller parameters not tuned for current column and hardware configuration
oven temperature sensor (thermocouple or RTD) degraded, displaced, or faulty
heater element partial failure causing uneven or intermittent heating
damaged oven door seal or insulation panels allowing heat loss and controller overcorrection
power supply fluctuation or ground fault affecting the heater control circuit
diagnostics: run isothermal method and log temperature vs. time — normal deviation is ± 0.1°C; overshoot > 2°C indicates PID issue
verify oven temperature against external calibrated thermocouple placed inside oven
run oven temperature ramp and compare setpoint vs. actual temperature trace in instrument software
inspect oven door seal and insulation panels for visible damage, gaps, or deformation
check instrument error log for heater fault codes or temperature sensor alarms
corrective_actions: run oven PID autotune procedure from instrument diagnostics menu (Agilent: Maintenance > Oven Calibration)
replace oven temperature sensor if external thermocouple comparison shows deviation > 2°C
replace oven door seal and insulation panels if heat loss is confirmed by thermal inspection
verify and stabilise laboratory power supply; use a dedicated circuit for the GC system
contact service engineer if heater element failure is confirmed — requires oven disassembly
escalation_conditions: oven temperature deviation > 5°C from setpoint after PID autotune — hardware fault requiring service
temperature sensor replacement does not resolve oscillation — heater or controller board failure

symptom: Increasing peak tailing, ghost peaks in blank runs, or gradual sensitivity loss after multiple injections
likely_causes: inlet liner contaminated with non-volatile sample matrix residues
septum degradation — septum bleed producing background peaks at elevated temperatures
column front section degraded from repeated injections of high-matrix samples
solvent with high boiling point causing flooding and residue deposition in liner
diagnostics: visually inspect liner under magnification for brown discolouration, deposits, or char
run a solvent blank injection and compare background chromatogram against reference blank
trim 15–30 cm from column inlet and rerun blank — improvement confirms column inlet contamination
check if ghost peaks have consistent elution temperatures matching septum bleed profile
corrective_actions: replace inlet liner and septum; use deactivated liners for trace-level analyses
bake column at 10°C below maximum rated temperature for 30–60 min under carrier gas flow
trim 15–30 cm from column inlet to remove contaminated section
switch to low-bleed septum (Merlin Microseal or equivalent) if septum bleed is confirmed
escalation_conditions: ghost peaks persist after full inlet maintenance and column bakeout — possible column damage or core contamination
sensitivity loss > 50% after inlet maintenance — detector or column end-of-life

symptom: System backpressure exceeds normal operating range, pressure alarm triggered, or flow rate drops unexpectedly
likely_causes: clogged column inlet frit from particulate matter accumulation
blocked in-line filter or guard column cartridge
mobile phase contains precipitated buffer salts or undissolved components
mobile phase viscosity too high due to low temperature or high aqueous content
flow rate set above column maximum specification for the column internal diameter
diagnostics: bypass column and measure system pressure alone — if high, blockage is in pump or tubing, not column
replace in-line guard column or filter and re-measure pressure
inspect mobile phase for turbidity, precipitate, or particles before and after filtration
back-flush column (if allowed by manufacturer) at 50% flow with strong solvent to test frit permeability
confirm column specifications match method flow rate and column internal diameter
corrective_actions: replace in-line filter or guard column cartridge as first corrective step
back-flush column at low flow rate if manufacturer protocol allows; replace column if pressure remains high
filter all mobile phases through 0.2 µm membrane filter before use
increase mobile phase temperature within column operating range to reduce viscosity
reduce pump flow rate to ≤ 80% of column maximum specification
escalation_conditions: system pressure exceeds pump maximum rated pressure — stop flow immediately to prevent damage
pressure remains high when column is removed — system-level blockage requires pump or tubing inspection

symptom: Asymmetric peaks with tailing on the later-eluting side, tailing factor greater than 1.5, reduced resolution
likely_causes: secondary silanol interactions between basic analyte and unreacted silanols on C18 stationary phase
void volume at column inlet from silica bed collapse or mechanical damage
extra-column dead volume in connections, injector, or detector flow cell
mobile phase pH outside optimal range for the column chemistry
column overloading — sample mass exceeds linear capacity causing asymmetric peak distortion
diagnostics: measure tailing factor using a low-concentration standard injection (< 1 µg on-column)
inject progressively smaller sample volumes — if tailing improves with dilution, column is overloaded
check all connection fittings for dead volume; measure from injector to detector with unretained marker
add 0.1% formic acid or 10 mM ammonium formate to mobile phase — silanophilic tailing improves with mobile-phase additives
run unretained marker (uracil) — broad or tailing void peak indicates dead volume at column inlet
corrective_actions: replace column if void at inlet confirmed (tailing unresponsive to additive and concentration changes)
add amine modifier to mobile phase (0.1% triethylamine) for basic analytes to reduce silanol interactions
dilute sample 5–10x; if tailing improves, reduce injection volume or switch to more dilute standard
re-make all column connections using correct ferrule size; replace worn compression fittings
switch to an end-capped C18 phase or a column with low silanol activity (e.g., BEH, HSS) for basic analytes
escalation_conditions: tailing factor > 2.5 with small injection volume on a new column — possible column batch defect
peak tailing prevents meeting USP system suitability tailing factor requirement (≤ 2.0)

symptom: Reduced MS signal for target analytes in complex matrix versus neat standard; inconsistent or inaccurate quantitation across sample types
likely_causes: co-eluting matrix components (salts, lipids, proteins) suppressing ionisation in ESI source
mobile phase additive concentration too high — TFA > 0.1% strongly suppresses ESI signal
phospholipid co-elution in biological matrices causing widespread signal suppression
injection volume too large, overloading the source with non-volatile matrix components
flow rate too high for ESI source — optimal ESI flow range is typically 0.1–0.5 mL/min
diagnostics: post-column infusion test: infuse pure standard via T-piece and inject blank matrix — signal dip identifies suppression window
compare stable isotope-labelled internal standard response in matrix versus neat standard
reduce injection volume 5-fold and compare signal normalised to injected mass
run HPLC-UV run with same gradient to visualise co-eluting UV-absorbing matrix components
switch mobile phase additive from TFA to 0.1% formic acid and compare signal
corrective_actions: optimise LC gradient to shift analyte elution away from matrix-dense region of the chromatogram
implement protein precipitation, LLE, or SPE cleanup to remove phospholipids and salts before injection
reduce TFA concentration to 0.01–0.05% or replace with 0.1% formic acid
add stable isotope-labelled internal standard co-eluting with analyte to correct for variable suppression
use online dilution or reduce injection volume to minimise matrix load on source
escalation_conditions: suppression > 50% in analyte elution window after matrix cleanup optimisation — method redesign required
quantitative accuracy fails validation criteria (> 15% bias from nominal) due to matrix effects

symptom: Sudden or gradual loss of GC-MS detector signal; reduced sensitivity or flat detector response across all analytes
likely_causes: detector filament broken or near end of service life
ion source contamination from column bleed or high-matrix sample residue
electron multiplier (SEM/CDEM) gain below usable threshold
vacuum system degraded — source operating at too high a pressure due to roughing pump wear
transfer line temperature too low causing analyte condensation before reaching source
diagnostics: run instrument autotune and review tune report: poor ion ratios or high mass offset indicates source contamination
check filament status in data system (Chemstation, Xcalibur, LabSolutions) — filament fault alert
monitor vacuum gauge: ion source vacuum should be < 1×10⁻⁵ Torr; high pressure indicates vacuum system issue
run PFTBA mass calibration standard and compare peak heights against last good tune report
verify transfer line temperature setpoint is at least equal to the maximum column oven temperature
corrective_actions: vent instrument and clean ion source (repeller, lenses, source block) per manufacturer cleaning protocol
replace filament if broken or if emission current in tune report is below specification
replace electron multiplier if detector gain < 10⁵ — service life typically 3–5 years
replace roughing pump oil or diaphragm pump diaphragm to restore vacuum
increase transfer line temperature to prevent condensation; set to 10–20°C above column maximum
escalation_conditions: source cleaning does not restore tune report to within specification — ion source component damaged
vacuum cannot reach operating pressure after pump maintenance — turbo pump failure requires service

symptom: System pressure exceeds operating limit or rises rapidly during gradient; instrument stops or triggers alarm
likely_causes: sub-2-µm column inlet frit clogged with sample particulates or buffer precipitate
column void from sub-2-µm particle collapse at high pressure
mobile phase not filtered or containing undissolved buffer salts
flow rate above column maximum (sub-2-µm columns typically rated to 600–1000 bar)
column temperature too low increasing mobile phase viscosity
diagnostics: measure system pressure without column (bypass) — if high, blockage is upstream of column
replace in-line filter (0.2 µm) before column and re-measure pressure
check mobile phase preparation: all salts fully dissolved, filtered through 0.2 µm
reduce flow rate by 30% and check if pressure falls proportionally
increase column temperature to 40–50°C to reduce viscosity-related pressure contribution
corrective_actions: replace in-line filter or guard column as immediate first step
filter all mobile phases through 0.2 µm PTFE membrane before pumping
reduce flow rate to within column operating specification
increase column temperature to reduce viscosity; most sub-2-µm columns tolerate 60–80°C
replace column if in-line filter replacement does not resolve elevated pressure
escalation_conditions: pressure exceeds instrument maximum (1000 bar for most UHPLC systems) — stop pump immediately
pressure remains high after column removal — pump seal or check valve damage, contact service

symptom: Suppressed conductivity baseline drifts upward or is noisy during isocratic or gradient ion chromatography run
likely_causes: suppressor regenerant exhausted or suppressor membrane fouled
eluent generator (EG) concentration unstable — KOH or MSA generator depleted
column not fully equilibrated with eluent before injection
sample anions or matrix contaminants fouling the suppressor
temperature fluctuation in column compartment affecting conductivity baseline
diagnostics: run blank eluent injection and observe baseline stability over 30 min — drift > 0.1 µS/cm indicates suppressor issue
check suppressor current and voltage in Chromeleon software — abnormal values indicate suppressor wear
verify EG cartridge concentration output by measuring eluent conductivity with external meter
check column equilibration volume — most ICS columns require 10–15 min at 1 mL/min before stable baseline
monitor column oven temperature stability — should be ± 0.1°C for stable conductivity
corrective_actions: regenerate suppressor per Dionex protocol: run deionised water for 30 min at full suppressor current
replace EG cartridge if eluent concentration deviates > 2% from setpoint
extend column equilibration time to 20–30 min before beginning sample sequence
clean suppressor with 5 mM oxalic acid (anion suppressor) per Thermo Fisher ICS maintenance protocol
recalibrate column oven temperature to ± 0.1°C; avoid placing instrument near air conditioning vents
escalation_conditions: baseline drift > 1 µS/cm over 10 min after full suppressor regeneration — suppressor membrane replacement needed
EG cartridge replacement does not restore eluent concentration — EG module fault, contact Thermo Fisher service

symptom: Weight loss step appears at unexpected temperature, spurious weight change events, or buoyancy artefact distorts baseline
likely_causes: buoyancy effect from purge gas density change with temperature — not corrected by empty-crucible baseline
moisture desorption from hygroscopic sample at 50–120°C masking or overlapping primary decomposition
sample preparation artefact: solvent inclusion, incomplete drying, or surface contamination
crucible not properly tared — balance offset from previous run
purge gas flow rate inconsistency changing apparent sample mass
diagnostics: run empty-crucible blank scan under identical conditions and subtract from sample scan
perform sample drying pre-treatment at 60–80°C for 2 h under vacuum before TGA to remove moisture
repeat scan with a fresh aliquot of the same material — reproducible step confirms real transition
verify purge gas flow rate with a flowmeter before measurement
compare crucible mass before loading to ensure correct tare value
corrective_actions: subtract matched empty-crucible baseline to remove buoyancy artefact
pre-dry sample at 60–80°C in vacuum oven for 2 h; analyse immediately after drying
use smaller sample mass (1–5 mg) to sharpen resolution and reduce crucible-contact effects
recalibrate balance using reference weights before sample sequence
maintain constant purge gas flow rate within ± 2 mL/min throughout run sequence
escalation_conditions: unexpected weight loss > 5% below 150°C on a nominally anhydrous sample after drying — likely solvent inclusion or impurity
buoyancy correction does not converge after baseline subtraction — hang-down wire or balance hardware issue

symptom: Diffraction peaks appear at incorrect 2θ positions; systematic offset from reference pattern or known d-spacings
likely_causes: sample surface not at correct goniometer height — sample displacement is the most common XRD systematic error
zero-point offset of detector or goniometer not calibrated; instrument drift since last alignment
sample preparation error: uneven surface, particles protruding above holder surface level
high sample absorption causing beam penetration below sample surface (transparency displacement)
thermal expansion of sample stage at elevated temperature shifting diffractometer geometry
diagnostics: run LaB6 or corundum (NIST SRM 676a) reference standard under identical conditions; compare measured vs. certified 2θ
calculate systematic shift: uniform Δ2θ across all peaks = zero offset or sample height error
if shift is non-linear (larger at low 2θ) = sample transparency or absorption displacement
check sample surface height using manufacturer alignment tool or laser height gauge
run goniometer alignment check in software (Bruker EVA, PANalytical HighScore, Rigaku SmartLab Studio)
corrective_actions: re-seat sample in holder; confirm surface is flush with holder rim; use back-fill or side-fill for powders
perform zero-point correction: run LaB6 standard, fit peaks, apply offset correction in software
perform full goniometer realignment following manufacturer procedure (PANalytical PIXcel, Bruker LynxEye)
for transparent samples (polymers, organics): use thin sample on zero-background holder
schedule service for optical realignment if LaB6 peaks still deviate > 0.03° 2θ after zero correction
escalation_conditions: peak position deviation > 0.05° 2θ from certified LaB6 values after zero correction — goniometer alignment required
phase identification fails in search-match software despite good peak shapes — systematic offset corrupts d-spacings

symptom: Diffraction peaks are unusually broad (large FWHM), poorly resolved from adjacent peaks, or show significant line broadening beyond instrument resolution
likely_causes: small crystallite size (< 100 nm) causing intrinsic Scherrer broadening — physical property of sample
microstrain broadening from lattice defects, dislocations, or residual stress
instrument broadening contribution: divergence or receiving slit too wide for the geometry
sample contains multiple phases or solid solutions with overlapping reflections
amorphous content diluting crystalline diffraction signal
diagnostics: measure FWHM of LaB6 or Si standard to determine instrument broadening contribution (instrument resolution function)
calculate apparent crystallite size using Scherrer equation: D = Kλ/(β cos θ), K = 0.9
perform Williamson-Hall plot (β cos θ vs. sin θ) to separate size and strain contributions
run TOPAS or HighScore Plus Rietveld refinement to deconvolute instrument vs. sample broadening
compare sample pattern with PDF-5+ reference card to identify additional phases
corrective_actions: if crystallite size is the cause: report Scherrer crystallite size — this is a real physical property
narrow divergence and receiving slits to reduce instrument broadening contribution
use Cu Kα1 monochromator (Ge(111) primary monochromator) to eliminate Kα2 and improve resolution
use Rietveld refinement to deconvolute overlapping phases (Bruker TOPAS, PANalytical HighScore Plus)
anneal sample to increase crystallite size if thermal treatment is acceptable for the material
escalation_conditions: FWHM > 5× instrument broadening even after Rietveld refinement — consult crystallographer for nanocrystalline analysis
Rietveld fit fails to converge despite correct phase model — possible unknown phase or severe preferred orientation

symptom: Diffraction peak intensities are weak, peak-to-background ratio is poor, or expected reflections are absent
likely_causes: X-ray tube aged or power too low — reduced flux output; tube life typically 5000–10000 h
preferred orientation — crystallites not randomly oriented, suppressing certain hkl reflections
sample too thin or insufficient irradiated volume for the selected divergence slit setting
sample absorbs X-rays strongly (high-Z elements, Fe-rich materials) causing self-absorption
incorrect divergence slit size: beam overspills sample edges wasting intensity
diagnostics: run corundum standard and compare peak intensities against historical laboratory baseline
check X-ray tube age and accumulated kWh in instrument log
measure generator high voltage and filament current; compare against setpoints
check if intensities match JCPDS/ICDD reference card — systematic absences suggest preferred orientation
pack sample fresh using side-loading or back-loading method to check if orientation changes result
corrective_actions: replace X-ray tube if intensity < 60% of reference standard at same conditions
increase generator power within tube rating (increase kV and mA proportionally)
re-prepare sample: grind to < 10 µm, use side-loading or back-loading to minimise preferred orientation
use spinner stage or capillary geometry to average crystallite orientations
for highly absorbing samples: use Mo or Ag X-ray source instead of Cu; or use transmission geometry
escalation_conditions: intensity drop > 40% vs. reference at same conditions after sample preparation check — X-ray tube replacement required
HV generator error message or tube instability alarm — service engineer required

symptom: Polydispersity index (PDI) consistently > 0.3; broad or multimodal size distribution; irreproducible size results
likely_causes: sample aggregation or agglomeration in the dispersant before or during measurement
dust or large particles contaminating cuvette or sample — even single dust particles dominate scattering
sample not properly dispersed: requires sonication, surfactant, or pH adjustment
genuinely polydisperse sample with inherent broad size distribution
measurement temperature not equilibrated — thermal gradients cause convection artifacts
diagnostics: inspect autocorrelation function (ACF): multimodal ACF = multiple populations; flat baseline required
examine size distribution by intensity, volume, and number — few large aggregates dominate intensity mean
filter sample through 0.2 µm and re-measure — if PDI drops dramatically, dust/aggregates are the cause
centrifuge at 2000–5000 × g for 5 min; measure supernatant
verify temperature equilibration ≥ 120 s before measurement start
corrective_actions: filter all samples and dispersant through 0.2 µm immediately before measurement
clean cuvette thoroughly: rinse 3× with filtered dispersant; use optical-quality cuvette free of scratches
sonicate in ultrasonic bath for 2–5 min then filter to break aggregates
optimise dispersant: adjust pH or add 0.05% Tween 20 to prevent aggregation
for PDI > 0.5: use CONTIN analysis or NanoSight NTA instead of cumulants fitting
escalation_conditions: PDI > 0.5 even after filtration and fresh dispersant — sample requires pre-fractionation (SEC or DGC) before DLS
count rate unstable and spiking > 20% during measurement — sample aggregating in real time, reformulate dispersant

symptom: Autocorrelation function (ACF) is flat or shows no significant decay; no size result; intercept near zero
likely_causes: sample too dilute — insufficient scattering intensity for autocorrelation signal
sample too concentrated — multiple scattering obscures single-particle signal
particles fully sedimented before or during measurement — no particles in beam path
particles too large (> 10 µm) — scattering fluctuation frequency too slow for DLS time scale
cuvette misalignment or laser path obstruction — sample not in beam focus
diagnostics: check count rate: optimal for Zetasizer ZS is 50–500 kcps; < 10 kcps = too dilute; > 1000 kcps = too concentrated
inspect cuvette for sedimented particles visible at bottom
confirm cuvette properly seated and lid closed; no trapped bubbles
check correlogram intercept: should be > 0.5 at 173° backscatter for reliable measurement
try 10× more concentrated sample to test if count rate improves
corrective_actions: adjust concentration: polymer/silica NPs 0.1–5 mg/mL; gold/silver NPs 0.01–0.1 nM
for sedimenting particles: mix immediately before measurement; use NanoSight NTA instead of DLS
re-seat cuvette; add sample slowly to avoid bubbles; set attenuator to automatic in Zetasizer software
dilute 10× if count rate > 1000 kcps; confirm PDI also improves
if count rate < 5 kcps at max laser power — sample below DLS detection limit, use alternative method
escalation_conditions: count rate < 5 kcps at maximum attenuator — sample below detection limit or fully sedimented
correlogram intercept < 0.1 after concentration optimisation — possible instrument laser alignment issue

symptom: Zeta potential shows high variability, unexpected sign reversal, or values outside expected range
likely_causes: DTS1070 electrode cell fouled with protein or lipid coating changing electrode response
ionic strength too high — double layer compression collapses zeta toward zero
incorrect measurement pH — zeta potential is strongly pH-dependent
sample concentration too high causing electrode polarisation
air bubbles in zeta cell capillary disrupting the applied electric field
diagnostics: run Malvern DTS1235 zeta reference standard (−42 ± 4.5 mV in pre-diluted reagent) — if off, cell is fouled
check dispersant conductivity: should be < 5 mS/cm; > 10 mS/cm compresses double layer
visually inspect DTS1070 electrode for protein film, discolouration, or damage
inspect capillary for bubbles: tap cell, inject slowly, check using Zetasizer auto-titrator protocol
measure same sample at 3 pH values to build zeta vs. pH profile and identify IEP
corrective_actions: replace DTS1070 disposable folded capillary cell after each protein or liposome measurement
dilute dispersant to < 5 mS/cm using 1 mM NaCl or 0.1 mM phosphate buffer for zeta work
prepare dispersant at controlled target pH using buffer; document pH alongside zeta result
dilute to < 1 mg/mL for proteins or < 0.5 mg/mL for NPs before zeta measurement
flush cell 3× with fresh dispersant to purge bubbles before sample injection
escalation_conditions: reference standard deviates > 10 mV from certified value after cell replacement — instrument service required
phase plot shows non-linear or erratic phase angle — electrode damage, replace cell immediately

symptom: Titrator fails to detect endpoint; runs to maximum volume without triggering; endpoint detected at wrong volume
likely_causes: pH or ISE electrode response sluggish or failed — contaminated liquid junction, cracked membrane, depleted reference electrolyte
incorrect titration parameters: EP recognition threshold too high, stop criterion not matching chemistry
wrong titrant concentration entered — causes endpoint at incorrect volume
titrant pump not priming or reservoir empty — no titrant actually delivered
sample chemistry incompatible with electrode type (e.g., pH electrode used for non-aqueous samples)
diagnostics: test electrode in calibration buffers pH 4, 7, 10 — slope should be > 56 mV/decade at 25°C
weigh delivered volume on balance and compare against nominal — confirms pump delivery
verify titrant concentration with certified primary standard (KHP for NaOH, Na2CO3 for HCl)
check liquid junction: unclog or refill KCl electrolyte if junction is dry or blocked
review Titrando/Titrino event log for error codes during the run
corrective_actions: recondition electrode overnight in 3 M KCl; replace reference electrolyte cartridge
replace electrode if slope < 50 mV/decade after reconditioning
restandardise titrant with 3 replicates of primary standard and update titer factor
prime burette manually and verify bubble-free delivery before starting sequence
for non-aqueous samples: use Solvotrode electrode with non-aqueous electrolyte (Metrohm part)
escalation_conditions: electrode replacement does not restore endpoint detection — verify sample matrix compatibility with method
burette gravimetric check error > 1% — exchange unit O-rings or pump seal replacement needed

symptom: High blank drift before titration; sample consumes abnormally high titre volume; results systematically high
likely_causes: CO2 absorption into NaOH titrant from atmosphere — increasing effective molarity or forming carbonate
electrode junction dried or contaminated causing unstable potential readings
sample not fully dissolved — slow reaction rate causing under-titration then back-drift
NaOH titrant aged > 2 weeks without CO2 protection
temperature drift in sample during titration changing equilibrium constant
diagnostics: measure blank drift rate: acceptable < 0.02 mL/min for aqueous acidimetry
check CO2 guard tube (Ascarite/soda lime) on NaOH burette — should be intact and recently replaced
titrate KHP primary standard and check recovery
compare duplicate titrations with different stir speeds to diagnose mass transfer issues
monitor temperature during titration — use thermostated vessel for high-precision work
corrective_actions: fit NaOH burette with fresh Ascarite guard tube; fresh NaOH solution deteriorates < 2 weeks without CO2 protection
restandardise NaOH using 3 replicates of KHP; update titer factor; discard solutions > 2 weeks old
increase stir speed or extend dissolution time; use kinetic titration mode for slow reactions (Metrohm: pause before EP)
replace electrode KCl electrolyte and unclog junction; store electrode in KCl between uses
use thermostated titration vessel (25 ± 0.5°C) for pharmaceutical or QC titrations
escalation_conditions: blank drift > 0.5 mL/min persists after burette protection — titrant contaminated, replace completely
KHP recovery < 99% or > 101% after restandardisation — investigate balance and weighing procedure

symptom: Titre volume consistently too high or too low versus expected; results outside acceptance criteria
likely_causes: titrant concentration inaccurate — wrong molarity at preparation or not restandardised
burette calibration drift — delivering incorrect volume
sample mass or volume measurement error — balance not calibrated, volumetric glassware misused
air bubble in burette line delivering less than nominal volume
incorrect molecular weight or purity factor in method calculation
diagnostics: titrate certified primary standard (KHP for NaOH, Na2CO3 for HCl) and calculate recovery factor
gravimetric burette check: weigh 1 mL delivered by burette — should be ≈ 1.000 g for water
verify sample mass on calibrated 4-place balance with OIML certified weights
inspect burette cylinder for air bubbles: prime 2× and visually confirm bubble-free
review method formula: correct molecular weight and titration factor
corrective_actions: restandardise titrant using 3 replicates of primary standard; update titer in Titrando method
perform gravimetric burette calibration; if error > 0.5%, replace Metrohm 20 mL exchange unit
re-calibrate balance with certified F1 or E2 weights before critical weighing operations
use only Class A volumetric glassware; clean with hot detergent water, rinse with deionised water
replace exchange unit piston O-rings if burette delivers inconsistently on repeat checks
escalation_conditions: burette error > 1% on gravimetric check after O-ring replacement — exchange unit requires service
restandardisation RSD > 0.2% across 3 replicates — systematic balance or weighing error

symptom: Karl Fischer titration shows high pre-titration drift; cell requires long conditioning; drift never stabilises below acceptable threshold
likely_causes: residual water in KF cell from previous high-moisture sample or insufficient conditioning
moisture ingress through exhausted desiccant or damaged seals — atmospheric water entering cell
KF reagent contaminated with water from hygroscopic solvent batch
electrode membrane contaminated or carbonised from previous reactive sample
temperature fluctuation in lab causing condensation on glassware or transfer components
diagnostics: measure drift rate: acceptable for volumetric KF < 0.1 mL/min; for coulometric < 5 µg/min
condition cell: run blank until drift < 0.05 mL/min (volumetric) or < 3 µg/min (coulometric)
check all seals, septum, and desiccant: silica gel should be blue/orange — pink/white = exhausted
inspect KF reagent: Hydranal Composite should be amber; brown-black = decomposition
check electrode: bipotentiometric electrode should polarise at 5–10 µA; open-circuit = electrode damaged
corrective_actions: condition cell: add 20–30 mL fresh working medium (methanol), titrate to dryness, repeat until drift stable
replace all desiccant on openings with fresh molecular sieves or silica gel; grease all glass joints
replace KF titrant if discoloured or water equivalent factor deviates > 2% from specification
clean bipotentiometric electrode with methanol-wetted cotton swab; platinum tip should be bright
replace Viton seals and O-rings in cell assembly if moisture ingress confirmed after desiccant replacement
escalation_conditions: drift does not stabilise after 3 conditioning cycles — possible cracked cell glassware or irreversibly fouled electrode
water equivalent factor deviates > 5% from labeled value — reagent lot issue, replace with new lot

symptom: Water content result lower than expected or reference method; inconsistent low results between replicates
likely_causes: slow sample dissolution — crystalline or waxy samples release water slowly in methanol working medium
sample reacts with KF reagent: aldehydes/ketones consume iodine; strongly basic samples neutralise acid medium
hygroscopic sample lost moisture between weighing and dissolution in cell
methanol working medium cannot dissolve sample — water trapped in undissolved particles
coulometric generator anode passivated — reduced electrochemical iodine generation efficiency
diagnostics: run Hydranal Water Standard 10.0 (≈ 10.00 mg/g water) — recovery should be 100 ± 1%
extend dissolution time 5–10 min with stirring before titration; check if result increases
for aldehydes/ketones: use Hydranal Composite 5K (ketone-free reagent)
for insoluble solids: use KF Oven method to transfer moisture via carrier gas
inspect coulometric generator electrode for brown/black iodine byproduct coating
corrective_actions: extend stir time to 5–15 min for slow-dissolving samples; gently warm working medium to 35–40°C
switch to specialised KF reagent: Hydranal-Composite 5K for ketones, Methanol Rapid for non-polar solvents
weigh samples in closed containers; add directly to KF cell via syringe to prevent pre-measurement moisture loss
for coulometric: clean generator electrode with 600-grit polishing paper; condition with 3 blank titrations
switch to KFO method for wax-like or poorly soluble solid samples
escalation_conditions: water standard recovery < 97% after reagent replacement — possible below-specification reagent lot
coulometric cell cleaning does not restore generator efficiency — generator electrode requires replacement

symptom: Coulometric Karl Fischer gives negative water result, erratic endpoint, or result much higher than expected
likely_causes: anolyte exhausted — iodide in anolyte depleted, cannot generate iodine electrochemically
strongly reducing sample (ascorbic acid, sulfite, H2S) reduces generated iodine back to iodide
generator electrode (platinum anode) passivated by organic deposits — reduced generation current
bipotentiometric endpoint electrode contaminated — false early endpoint detection
catholyte consumed or contaminated — H2 evolution interfering with endpoint
diagnostics: run Hydranal Water Standard 5 mg/g: recovery outside 98–102% confirms reagent or electrode issue
observe generation current in Titrando display: should peak then decay; flat curve = no generation
check anolyte colour: light amber = normal; dark brown-black = iodine accumulated or anolyte exhausted
for negative results: reducing agent consuming iodine faster than generated
inspect both platinum electrodes visually: dark coating = passivation
corrective_actions: replace anolyte and catholyte (Hydranal Coulomat AG and CG) if water standard fails or anolyte discoloured
for reducing agents: use Hydranal-Coulomat A-Olio; for SO2/H2S: use specific reagent formulation
clean generator electrode with 600-grit polishing paper; rinse with fresh anolyte; run 3 conditioning blank titrations
for redox-interfering samples: switch to volumetric KF which is less sensitive to redox interference
replace bipotentiometric electrode if platinum pins are corroded or visibly damaged
escalation_conditions: water standard recovery < 95% after full reagent and electrode replacement — cell component damage
generation current does not respond to water injection — generator circuit fault, contact Metrohm service

symptom: KF Oven result lower than expected; result increases with longer drying time suggesting incomplete water transfer from sample to KF cell
likely_causes: oven temperature too low — bound water not fully released at set temperature
drying time too short for sample mass and thermal stability
carrier gas (dry N2) flow rate too low — water vapour not swept completely to KF cell
transfer line temperature too low — water condenses inside the glass or PTFE tube before reaching KF cell
sample mass too large — centre does not reach oven temperature within the drying time
diagnostics: run cellulose KF standard (≈ 5% water) at same temperature and time — recovery indicates transfer efficiency
extend drying time from default (3–5 min) to 8–10 min; compare results
vary oven temperature in 20°C steps while monitoring cumulative result — plateau = complete release temperature
verify carrier gas flow: typically 100–150 mL/min dry N2; use flowmeter to confirm
check transfer line for condensation: unheated glass tube > 30 cm can condense water from humid samples
corrective_actions: increase oven temperature in 20°C steps until result plateaus — typical optimal range: 100–200°C for pharmaceutical solids
increase drying time until successive 1-min increments add < 0.5% additional water
increase carrier gas flow to 150 mL/min; use freshly dried N2 from gas generator or 4A molecular sieve column
heat or insulate transfer line to ≥ 10°C above oven temperature to prevent condensation
reduce sample mass to ≤ 0.5 g for solid samples to improve heat penetration
escalation_conditions: result does not plateau even at maximum oven temperature — sample decomposition interfering at all temperatures
cellulose standard recovery < 95% after optimisation — check oven heater element and transfer line integrity

symptom: Blank run with empty sample boat gives high water reading; baseline moisture transfer too high for accurate low-moisture measurements
likely_causes: residual moisture in transfer line from previous high-moisture sample or insufficient purging
carrier gas (N2) not sufficiently dry — moisture from gas supply entering the oven cell
sample boat contaminated with atmospheric moisture before measurement
septum or seals on oven connection not sealing properly — ambient humid air ingress
KF cell working medium absorbing atmospheric moisture during inter-run idle time
diagnostics: run 3 consecutive blanks (empty boat): decreasing blank indicates carryover from previous run
measure carrier gas dew point: should be < −50°C for measurements below 0.1% water
run blank after 20 min N2 purge — should approach 0.2–0.5 µg H2O/min maximum
compare blank before and after inserting empty sample boat — boat contribution should be < 0.1 µg
inspect septum and glass joints for cracks or misalignment
corrective_actions: purge system with dry N2 for 20–30 min before starting measurements
replace N2 drying tube (molecular sieve 4A or Sicapent) if dew point > −40°C
pre-condition sample boats: heat at 150°C for 30 min; store in desiccator until use
replace septum on oven inlet if blank does not decrease after purging
keep KF cell covered with desiccant-protected cap between measurements
escalation_conditions: blank does not decrease below 2 µg/min after 30 min purging and septum replacement — system-level leak requiring service
carrier gas dew point cannot be dried below −30°C — drying column exhausted or supply contaminated

symptom: Sample chars, decomposes, or shows discolouration in KF oven boat; combustion products interfere with KF endpoint; results erratic
likely_causes: oven temperature exceeds sample thermal decomposition temperature — organic matrix decomposes releasing interfering species
low-melting sample components flow and contact oven wall causing local overheating
residual oxygen in carrier gas or insufficient N2 purge causing partial combustion
volatile organic acids or aldehydes released on thermal treatment react with KF working medium
sample mass too large — centre reaches decomposition temperature before water fully evolved
diagnostics: visually inspect sample boat after oven run — char or colour change indicates decomposition
run TGA on sample to identify exact decomposition temperature and distinguish water loss from decomposition
compare results at 100, 120, 150°C — if results increase then drop, decomposition interferes above a threshold
check KF working medium colour after run: brown/black = organic decomposition products entered cell
run N2 purity check: O2 content should be < 10 ppm for combustion-sensitive samples
corrective_actions: reduce oven temperature to just above complete water release temperature (use TGA to identify optimal window)
reduce sample mass to 50–150 mg to limit decomposition product load
increase N2 purge time to ≥ 5 min before oven heating to eliminate residual O2
use glass wool plug in sample boat to hold powders and improve heat distribution
if charring unavoidable: switch to direct KF titration in suitable solvent or headspace moisture method
escalation_conditions: KF working medium persistently brown/black after runs — replace cell contents completely
sample fuses to boat or oven wall — oven requires dismantling and cleaning; contact Metrohm service
